title: Go Type Switches
content:
### Type Switches

A type switch is a cleaner way to do multiple type assertions.

### Basic Syntax

```go
func describe(v any) {
    switch v := v.(type) {
    case int:
        fmt.Printf("Integer: %d\n", v)
    case string:
        fmt.Printf("String: %s (len=%d)\n", v, len(v))
    case float64:
        fmt.Printf("Float: %.2f\n", v)
    case bool:
        fmt.Printf("Boolean: %t\n", v)
    default:
        fmt.Printf("Unknown type: %T\n", v)
    }
}
```

### Variable Shadowing

```go
func check(v any) {
    switch val := v.(type) {
    case int:
        // val is int here
        fmt.Println(val * 2)
    case string:
        // val is string here
        fmt.Println(len(val))
    default:
        // val is still any here
        fmt.Println("unknown")
    }
}
```

### Multiple Types in One Case

```go
func isNumeric(v any) bool {
    switch v.(type) {
    case int, int8, int16, int32, int64:
        return true
    case uint, uint8, uint16, uint32, uint64:
        return true
    case float32, float64:
        return true
    case complex64, complex128:
        return true
    }
    return false
}
```

### Nil Case

```go
func checkNil(v any) {
    switch v.(type) {
    case nil:
        fmt.Println("is nil")
    case string:
        fmt.Println("is string")
    }
}
```

### Type Switch with Interface

```go
func isStringer(v any) bool {
    switch v.(type) {
    case fmt.Stringer:
        return true
    }
    return false
}
```

### Comparison: Type Switch vs Type Assertions

| Aspect | Type Switch | Type Assertions |
|--------|-------------|-----------------|
| Readability | Clean for 3+ types | Verbose |
| Variable typing | Typed per case | One type at a time |
| Default case | Yes | No |
| Multiple types per case | Yes | No |

> **Key Point:** Type switches are preferred over chained type assertions when checking 3 or more types. The variable in each case takes on the concrete type automatically.
codeExample:
package main

import "fmt"

func inspect(v any) {
    switch val := v.(type) {
    case nil:
        fmt.Println("nil")
    case int:
        fmt.Printf("int %d (double: %d)\n", val, val*2)
    case string:
        fmt.Printf("string %q (len: %d)\n", val, len(val))
    case []int:
        fmt.Printf("[]int %v\n", val)
    default:
        fmt.Printf("unknown %T\n", val)
    }
}

func main() {
    inspect(nil)
    inspect(42)
    inspect("hello")
    inspect([]int{1, 2, 3})
    inspect(3.14)
}

quiz.question: What keyword is used in a type switch?
Can multiple types share a single case in a type switch?
options: No, each type needs its own case
